food_subgroup: Crustaceans — whole crab platter, typically steamed or stir-fried with Thai sauces

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mudcrab platter; FOODON_03411433 (crab food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crab) is the closest parent; no specific mudcrab platter composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 (Crab, food) is the closest available concept; no mudcrab platter composite concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A mudcrab platter is typically served as a whole or halved large crab with sauces and sides, with an edible portion of approximately 500 g per serving for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15139); adapted for mudcrab (Scylla serrata) with reference to FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for crustaceans
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry for mudcrab platter as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (blue crab, moist heat) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Scylla serrata (mudcrab). Platter-level values reflect the crab meat component; sauce contributions to sodium and fat are noted in the notes field. Per-100g values represent the edible cooked crab meat portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC); shellfish and crustaceans analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mudcrab platter. Crustaceans and shellfish are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with other cooked shellfish and crustaceans, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafood. Glycemic load per 500 g serving is approximately 0.2 given carbohydrate_g of 0.9 per 100g (GL = 0.9 × 5 / 100 × 5 = 0.23, rounded to 0.2).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from crab meat and Thai sauces (fish sauce, oyster sauce)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with mudcrab platter due to its elevated sodium content (approximately 395 mg per 100 g from crab meat alone, rising significantly with Thai sauce additions); portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mudcrab platter is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it is very low in carbohydrates (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g) with a negligible glycemic load, making it unlikely to cause postprandial blood glucose elevation when consumed without high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol content and sodium load from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While mudcrab is a lean protein source with low saturated fat, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size due to moderate dietary cholesterol (approximately 78 mg per 100 g) and the high sodium contributed by Thai cooking sauces, which may adversely affect blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mudcrab Platter is a premium Thai-style seafood dish featuring whole or halved mudcrab (Scylla serrata), a large estuarine crab prized for its sweet, dense, and succulent meat. The crab is typically steamed, stir-fried, or grilled and served as a platter with Thai aromatics such as garlic, chilli, lemongrass, and sauces including fish sauce, oyster sauce, and black pepper sauce or butter-based preparations. The flesh is firm, moist, and rich in umami flavour, with a slightly briny sweetness characteristic of mudcrab. Nutritionally, mudcrab is an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 19 g per 100 g), vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus, while being very low in carbohydrates and fat. The dish is clinically relevant for its high protein density and negligible glycemic load, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the sodium content from Thai sauces warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, mudcrab platters are a restaurant centrepiece, often priced at a premium due to the size and quality of the crab. The dish is associated with celebratory dining and is commonly served at Thai seafood restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a mudcrab platter?
Can I eat mudcrab if I have high blood pressure?
What is mudcrab platter made of?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS entry exists for mudcrab platter as a composite Thai restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab data and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA crustacean data for Scylla serrata. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the crab meat component is well-characterised in approved sources; the main uncertainty is the sauce contribution to sodium and fat in the as-served platter.
notes: 1. Per-100g values represent the edible cooked mudcrab meat component. Sauce contributions (fish sauce, oyster sauce, butter, black pepper sauce) will increase sodium, fat, and energy in the as-served dish; actual platter sodium may be 500–800 mg per 100 g of total edible matter including sauce. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA crustacean data. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for cooked crab. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in cooked crustacean meat per USDA SR Legacy. 5. sugar_g is 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible in cooked crab meat per USDA SR Legacy — these are naturally zero for plain cooked crustacean flesh. 6. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with cooked shellfish and crustaceans from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mudcrab platter exists. 7. Halal status is marked false as Thai-style preparations typically include oyster sauce and fish sauce whose halal certification varies by restaurant. 8. The cuisine tag in the input is Thai; the dish is served at a Thai seafood restaurant in Singapore. Multilingual names include Thai script as mandatory per G6. Chinese names are included as the dish is commonly ordered by Chinese-speaking diners in Singapore. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish; the mandatory Singaporean multilingual requirement (G6) applies only to Singaporean dishes, not Thai dishes served in Singapore.
